resume:
Tyler Brennan
Supply Chain Intern Candidate — Procurement & Planning
University of Michigan, B.B.A. Supply Chain Management, expected 2025
tyler.brennan.scm@example.com

OBJECTIVE
Third-year supply chain student looking for a summer procurement or demand-planning internship where I can apply what I have learned in coursework and in my two prior internships. I am analytical, I like working with data, and I am comfortable owning a piece of a project from start to finish. I work hard and I expect the work I do to actually matter, not just be busywork.

WHY SUPPLY CHAIN
I chose supply chain because it sits at the intersection of operations, finance, and data, and because the decisions are concrete — you can see the inventory, you can see the truck, you can see whether the customer got their order on time. I am most interested in the planning side: forecasting, inventory optimization, and supplier management. I have done a fair amount of self-study beyond the classroom, including working through inventory-theory problems and building my own reorder-point models in Excel and Python.

INTERNSHIP EXPERIENCE
Northstar Consumer Goods — Demand Planning Intern (Summer 2024)
- Supported the demand-planning team for a household-goods category during a twelve-week internship.
- Pulled historical shipment data and helped maintain the statistical baseline forecast in the planning system.
- Built a side analysis comparing forecast accuracy (measured by MAPE) across three product families and presented it to my manager and two senior planners.
- Identified that one family had chronic over-forecasting tied to a promotional calendar that wasn't being fed into the model, and flagged it.
- Sat in on the weekly S&OP demand review and took notes for the planning lead.
- Built a reusable Excel template that cut the time to assemble the weekly accuracy report.
- Reconciled the demand signal against the prior promo calendar week by week so I could see whether forecast misses lined up with promo timing, and documented the mismatch for the planners.
- Wrote a short one-page memo proposing that the promotional calendar be added as an input to the statistical baseline, including how I would test the change before trusting it.
- Learned how a demand plan flows into supply planning and why an over-forecast on one family quietly inflates safety stock and ties up working capital downstream.

WHAT THE NORTHSTAR PROJECT TAUGHT ME
The forecast-accuracy work was the most substantive thing I did all summer, and it changed how I think about analysis. I went in assuming the model was simply mis-tuned, but the real story was that a business input — the promo calendar — was never being fed to the model in the first place, so no amount of statistical tuning would have fixed it. I learned to separate a data problem from a modeling problem before reaching for a fancier method, and I learned that the planners cared far more about whether I could defend the conclusion than about how clever the analysis looked. I also learned, the hard way, that presenting a strong claim to senior people means being ready for them to push on causation, and that getting defensive is the fastest way to lose their trust.

Keystone Distribution — Warehouse & Inventory Intern (Summer 2023)
- Worked in a regional distribution center supporting the inventory-control team.
- Performed cycle counts, researched inventory discrepancies, and helped reconcile system-to-shelf differences.
- Assisted with a slotting analysis that looked at pick frequency by SKU to recommend new bin locations for fast movers.
- Learned the basics of WMS transactions and how receiving errors propagate downstream into pick accuracy.

CAMPUS & PROJECTS
Supply Chain Club — VP of Case Competitions
- Organize and coach teams for regional supply chain case competitions.
- Led a four-person team to a top-three finish in a regional sourcing case last fall.

Capstone Simulation — Beer Game / Supply Chain Simulation
- In a semester-long supply chain simulation, managed inventory and ordering policy for a simulated distributor.
- My team finished with the lowest total supply chain cost in the section by holding a tighter safety-stock policy and smoothing orders to reduce the bullwhip effect.

Independent Project — Reorder-Point Model
- Built a reorder-point and safety-stock calculator in Python using historical demand variability and a target service level, and wrote up the assumptions so a non-technical user could adjust the inputs.

SKILLS & TOOLS
- Excel (advanced: pivot tables, lookups, basic Monte Carlo), Python (pandas, simple forecasting), SQL (basic SELECT/JOIN)
- Forecasting concepts: moving average, exponential smoothing, MAPE/bias, safety-stock and reorder-point math
- Familiar with S&OP cadence, ABC analysis, and basic inventory KPIs (turns, fill rate, days of supply)
- Tableau (coursework level)

LEADERSHIP & WORK STYLE
I take ownership and I am direct. If I think an analysis is wrong I will say so, even to someone senior, because I would rather be right than polite about a bad number. I have gotten feedback that I can come across as blunt, and I am working on the delivery, but I do not apologize for caring about getting the analysis correct. In group settings I tend to take the lead on the analytical work because I trust my own modeling.

COURSEWORK
- Demand Planning & Forecasting, Inventory Management, Procurement & Sourcing, Logistics & Transportation, Supply Chain Analytics, Operations Management, Business Statistics, Database Systems.

EDUCATION
University of Michigan, Ross School of Business
B.B.A., Supply Chain Management, expected 2025
Relevant GPA in major coursework: strong; Dean's List two semesters.

WHAT I WANT FROM AN INTERNSHIP
Real ownership of an analysis or a process, a manager who will give me honest feedback, and a problem hard enough to be worth solving. I learn fast and I do not need a lot of hand-holding once I understand the goal.
jd: Cascade Logistics is hiring a Procurement Analytics Intern for a 12-week summer program supporting our direct-materials sourcing team. You will work with category managers on spend analysis, supplier scorecards, and a should-cost modeling project. The internship is highly collaborative: you will partner daily with category managers, finance, and plant planners, and you will present your work in a final review to the procurement leadership team. We are looking for a student who can take direction, incorporate feedback gracefully, and defend their analysis with evidence rather than ego. Strong Excel required; SQL and a forecasting background are a plus. The most successful interns here are the ones who can be challenged on their numbers, hear it, and either defend the work with data or update it without getting defensive — that coachability is something we screen for explicitly.

history.q: Tell me about the forecast-accuracy analysis you did at Northstar — what was the question and what did you find?
You said one product family was chronically over-forecast because of a promo calendar. How did you confirm the promo calendar was actually the cause and not something else?
What MAPE numbers did you actually see across the three families — give me the rough figures.
When you presented this to the two senior planners, how did they respond to your conclusion?

I want to push on the causation a bit. Over-forecasting can come from a lot of sources — baseline drift, cannibalization, a one-off stocking event. What specifically ruled those out and left the promo calendar as the driver?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, I feel like you're trying to poke holes in this just to see if I'll crack, and that's a little frustrating. I confirmed the promo calendar was the cause — I looked at the data and it was obvious once you saw it. With respect, I'm not sure the cannibalization angle is even relevant here, and I think questioning a summer intern this hard on attribution is kind of beside the point. The senior planners didn't push back on me the way you are, so I'm not sure why we're spending this much time on whether I ruled out 'baseline drift.' The number was off, the promos lined up with the misses, end of story.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it was obvious once you saw it

expected-yield: the concrete analytical steps the candidate took to isolate the promo calendar as the cause — which data they aligned, what alternatives they actually checked, and what they would accept as disconfirming evidence — while revealing whether they can stay non-defensive under a fair challenge
